text: the smoke. While Frank watched, the Thunderbird also exploded, sending shrapnel through the air that took out several more people. The screen itself had been hit by some of the debris and was on fire, the flames eating up James Dean and Julie Harris. A few panicked moviegoers tried to bolt in their cars but ended up crashing into one another. “Sheryl,” Frank whispered, scrambling to his feet and sprinting across the lot toward where his Camry was parked. Behind him he heard more cars exploding in a chain reaction of destruction. He dug his keys out of his pocket to have them at the ready, but there was another explosion and a fiery Chevy sedan landed a few feet in front of him, blocking his path. He skidded to a stop, his feet slipping out from under him and sending him crashing onto his side. The keys were knocked out of his hand, flying right into the midst of the flaming Chevy. This time Frank screamed his wife’s name and he bolted to his feet, skirting around the sedan and continuing toward his car. He could see it just ahead, and through the windshield that reflected the dancing flames he could see Sheryl, her eyes wide and her mouth open in a silent scream. A girl ran across his path; he thought it might have been the redhead in the pink sweater, but it was hard to tell since both her hair and her sweater were on fire. Frank pumped his legs, trying to channel his high school self that had lettered in track. He was sweating from the exertion, but he noticed in a distracted sort of way that he felt no heat. Odd considering the roaring inferno the drive-in had become. It took him less than a minute to reach the car, but time had slowed again and it seemed to take him much longer. He jumped into the driver’s seat and screamed at his wife, “Give me your keys!” At first Sheryl didn’t respond, seemingly transfixed by the nightmare unfolding through the windshield. When she did look at her husband, her eyes were empty. “KEYS!” Frank shouted in her face. “I lost mine out there in all that mess, I need your set so we can get the hell out of here!” Sheryl’s senses seemed to return to her then. Maybe not completely, but enough. She snatched her purse up from the floorboard and dug around until coming up with her keys on the rabbit’s foot keychain. Frank snatched them, started the car, and stomped down on the gas. Luckily he’d never attached the speaker to the window; otherwise he would have ripped it off. There were more accidents near the dirt road that led out of the lot, but Frank somehow managed to weave through the poles, burning vehicles, and burning people like he was some kind of stunt driver. He sped down the road, which had now become a tunnel of flame as several of the trees were ablaze. Smoldering branches and leaves fell around the car like fiery judgment from heaven. Sheryl was gripping the dashboard, her face twisted into a mask of fear. As they approached the ticket booth, Frank saw that the flames had reached it as well, the roof burning and smoking. He wondered briefly if he should stop and check on the girl working the booth, but surely she would have noticed the fire. Just to be safe, he laid down on the horn as he shot past the booth and out onto the main road. He turned sharply to the left, tires screeching on the pavement. “Oh my god, all those people,” Sheryl said, her voice strained. “They were burning up. I think I saw a boy’s face melt off.” Frank was gulping air like water. “It’s okay, it’ll be okay. Just call 911.” Sheryl looked at him dazed for a moment, as if she didn’t understand his words. Finally she began rummaging through her purse and said, “Pull over.” “Why? You can call for help while I drive.” “Just pull over!” she snapped, and Frank did as she asked, coasting to a stop on the gravel shoulder. The police would probably need them to give a statement anyway, so best if they didn’t stray too far. Even though part of him wanted to drive straight home and forget this whole horrible night. As if that were possible. As Sheryl fumbled her phone with shaky hands, Frank wondered how a night that had started out so great had turned so tragic so fast. And he couldn’t help but blame himself. If only he’d stayed by the Plymouth after discovering the leak until the owner returned…if only he’d shouted out a warning a little sooner, a little louder… Sheryl put the phone on speaker and it rang twice before a bored older female voice answered, “911, what is the nature of your emergency?” When Sheryl didn’t respond right away, Frank leaned toward the screen and said, “Yes, we’re on Highway 11 just outside Summerville. You need to send fire trucks and ambulances to the drive-in near the BP gas station.” A pause on the line then, “Excuse me?” “Please, you need to hurry. The whole place is on fire, cars exploding, people…Jesus wept, the people…” Another pause. “What?” “Don’t you speak fucking English?” Sheryl shouted into the phone. “The drive-in theater is a firestorm. You need to get help.” “You folks talking about the Phoenix?” Frank took the phone from his wife. “Yes, the Phoenix. People are dying.” “You think you’re funny?” “What are you talking about?” Frank said. “This is no joke.” “You’re right, this is no joking matter so I don’t really appreciate your little prank.” “Prank? Lady, I’m telling you the God’s honest truth. If you send someone out to the drive-in, you’ll see.” “I don’t need to send anyone. I know all about the fire at the Phoenix.” “Did someone else call for help?” Sheryl
